title: Tambaroora Jim
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never drew a sword to fight a dozen foes alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor gave a life to save a life no better than his own.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He lived because he had been born, the hero of my song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fought the battle with his fist wheneן¿½er he fought a wrong.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet there are many men who would do anything for him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A simple chap as went by name of ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He used to keep a shanty in the ן¿½Come-and-find-it Scrub,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there were few but knew the name of Tambarooraן¿½s pub.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He wasnן¿½t great in lambing down, as many landlords are,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never was a man less fit to stand behind a bar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Off-hand, as most bush natives are, and freckled, tall, and slim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A careless native of the land was ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When people said that loafers took the profit from his pub,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d ask them how they thought a chap could do without his grub;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d say, ן¿½Iן¿½ve gone for days myself without a bite or sup,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh! Iן¿½ve been through the mill and know what ן¿½tis to be hard-up.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He might have made his fortune, but he wasnן¿½t in the swim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For no one had a softer heart than ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One dismal day I tramped across the Come-and-find-it Flats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With ן¿½Ballarat Adolphusן¿½ and a mate of ן¿½Ballaratן¿½sן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas nearly night and raining fast, and all our things were damp,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½d no tobacco, and our legs were aching with the cramp;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We couldnן¿½t raise a cent, and so our lamp of hope was dim;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus we struck the shanty kept by ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We dropped our swags beneath a tree, and squatted in despair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Jim came out to watch the rain, and saw us sitting there;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He came and muttered, ן¿½I suppose you havenן¿½t half -a-crown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But come and get some tucker, and a drink to wash it down.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so we took our blueys up and went along with him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then we knew why bushmen swore by ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We sat beside his kitchen fire and nursed our tired knees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blessed him when we heard the rain go rushing through the trees.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He made us stay, although he knew we couldnן¿½t raise a bob,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tuckered us until we made some money on a job.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And many times since then weן¿½ve filled our glasses to the brim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And drunk in many pubs the health of ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A man need never want a meal while Jim had ן¿½junkן¿½ to carve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ן¿½Tambarooraן¿½ always said a fellow couldnן¿½t starve.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this went on until he got a bailiff in his pub,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through helping chaps as couldnן¿½t raise the money for their grub.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so, one rainy evening, as the distant range grew dim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He humped his bluey from the Flats, did ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I miss the fun in Jimן¿½s old bar, the laughter and the noise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The jolly hours I used to spend on pay-nights with the boys.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thatן¿½s all past, and vain regrets are useless, Iן¿½ll allow;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They say the Come-and-find-it Flats are all deserted now.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor ן¿½Tambarooraן¿½sן¿½ dead, perhaps, but thatן¿½s all right with him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Peter cottons on to chaps like ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I trust that he and I may meet where starry fields are grand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And liquor up together in the pubs in spirit-land.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But if you chance to drop on Jim while in the West, my lad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You wonן¿½t forget to tell him that I want to see him bad.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I want to shake his hand again, I want to shout for him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I want to have a glass or two with ן¿½Tambaroora Jim.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
